judgments.comparative_verdict: Report 1 covers all four expectations, including correctly flagging the RESULTS_DB collision risk and recommending an env-var override, whereas Report 2 explicitly dismisses that same finding as "dead config" and "not required," missing expectation (d); on (a)-(c) the two are comparable.

report_1.evidence: | pipeline/registry.py | F-02, F-05, F-09 | ... Engineer A — config & prompts: F-02, F-05, F-09 (`pipeline/registry.py`) + F-03, F-10 ... I bundled registry + prompts into one engineer rather than splitting further

report_2.evidence: Group 2 — pipeline/registry.py (F‑02, F‑05, F‑09 — 3 findings, one 11-line file)... three people rebasing against the same tiny file at once is avoidable overhead.

report_1.evidence: Wave 1 (parallel, 3 engineers, start immediately): Engineer A — config & prompts: F-02, F-05, F-09 ... Engineer B — rubric parsing: F-01 ... then F-11 ... Engineer C — batch runner: owns pipeline/run_batch.py outright

report_2.evidence: Groups 1–4 touch four disjoint files, so they can genuinely start in parallel, wave 1 ... F‑11 (new edge-case test) should be written alongside/against the fixed behavior, TDD-style, by the same engineer.

report_1.evidence: "Tighten" is a rubric/quality-bar policy call, and picking numbers is exactly the kind of guess the project documentation ... says to escalate rather than make. I would route this to whoever owns the scoring rubric for explicit new values (or a target pass-rate to back-solve from) before any code is touched.

report_2.evidence: This is a scoring-policy call with pipeline-wide blast radius (it changes every score the pipeline emits) and currently zero regression test guards the existing behavior. This needs sign-off from whoever owns rubric/scoring policy, not an engineer's best guess, and should land with a new test locking in the agreed values.

report_1.evidence: A private override for RESULTS_DB (tests/conftest.py:4), e.g. exporting RESULTS_DB=/tmp/<name>-results.db, instead of relying on the shared default /var/tmp/llm-scoring/results.db, so that if a test ever does write there, parallel engineers don't collide.

report_2.evidence: Not required, but worth knowing about: tests/conftest.py:4 defines RESULTS_DB = os.environ.get("RESULTS_DB", "/var/tmp/llm-scoring/results.db"). I grepped for other uses and found none — it's currently dead config, so no one needs to stand up a real results database to run today's suite.
